critic_raw:
The most critical flaw is explicit test set contamination. Section 6 states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' This directly violates test set integrity — the test set is used for model selection decisions, and any 'discrepancy-based adjustment' means the final reported test performance reflects a model chosen partly based on test set feedback. This is not a holdout evaluation; it is a multi-round test set consultation.

The RNN receives sequential visit data (padded/truncated to 12 prior visits with diagnosis codes, procedure codes, medication classes, lab summaries) while the logistic regression baseline receives only demographic and clinical summary features without sequential structure. The models have fundamentally different information access — the RNN exploits visit history trajectories while LR gets point-in-time summaries. Any observed advantage cannot be attributed to RNN architecture vs. LR; it is confounded by sequential information access.

Preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting. For a readmission model where the vocabulary of diagnosis and procedure codes changes as new ICD-10/CPT codes are added over 5 years of EHR data, fitting vocabulary on the full dataset means test-period codes are included in the training vocabulary — this is not a major source of leakage but the normalization statistics leakage is real.

Stratified random sampling across 5 years of EHR data ignores temporal autocorrelation between visits from the same patient. A patient with 10 admissions over 5 years could have visits 1-9 in training and visit 10 in test — the RNN trained on visits 1-9 effectively memorizes this patient's history and evaluates against visit 10. This is not future-unseen evaluation; it is within-patient interpolation masquerading as generalization.

Truncating sequences to 12 visits discards longitudinal history for frequent utilizers (the highest-risk patients). The RNN is being evaluated precisely on the patients where its architectural advantage should be strongest, but those patients' histories are truncated to 12 visits — this may systematically understate RNN benefit.
all_issues_raised: Section 6 explicitly states the test set is used after validation hyperparameter tuning to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' — this is direct test set contamination for model selection, invalidating the holdout evaluation.

The RNN receives full sequential visit trajectories while logistic regression receives only demographic and clinical summary features — the comparison confounds RNN architecture with sequential information access, so any observed advantage cannot be attributed to model type.
Preprocessing vocabularies and scalers are fit on the complete dataset including test-period records — test-period diagnosis/procedure code distributions inform training-set vocabulary construction.

Stratified random splitting of patient visit sequences allows visits from the same patient to appear in both training and test — the model effectively memorizes patient-specific trajectories rather than generalizing to new patients.

Sequence truncation to 12 visits discards history for high-frequency utilizers (the highest-risk patients), which may systematically understate the RNN's capacity advantage precisely in the patient population where sequential modeling should matter most.

Preprocessing vocabularies and scalers are fit on the complete dataset including test-period records — test-period diagnosis/procedure code distributions inform training-set vocabulary construction.

Stratified random splitting of patient visit sequences allows visits from the same patient to appear in both training and test — the model effectively memorizes patient-specific trajectories rather than generalizing to new patients.
